items.q: Why do some rivers form deltas and others form estuaries?
items.correct: Deltas form where rivers deposit sediment faster than the sea can remove it (calm seas, heavy load); estuaries form where tidal energy removes sediment (strong tides).
items.misconception: Deltas form on big rivers and estuaries on small ones.
items.random_wrong: Deltas form in cold climates and estuaries in warm climates.
items.why: Size doesn't determine the form. The balance of sediment supply vs marine energy does — the Nile (delta) and Thames (estuary) are similar size but in different sea conditions.

items.q: What does Frank's dependency theory argue?
items.correct: That LICs (periphery) are kept poor by exploitative trade relationships with HICs (core) — underdevelopment is actively produced by capitalism, not a stage that countries grow out of.
items.misconception: Dependency theory says LICs are poor because they depend too much on foreign aid.
items.random_wrong: Dependency theory is the same as Rostow's modernisation theory.
items.why: Dependency theory is structural and critical, opposing Rostow. The 'dependency' is on the global economic system, not aid. Students confuse the word 'dependency' with aid reliance, missing the political-economic critique.

items.q: What are the three cells of global atmospheric circulation?
items.correct: The Hadley cell (equator to 30 degrees), Ferrel cell (30 to 60 degrees) and Polar cell (60 degrees to poles) together redistribute heat from equator to poles.
items.misconception: There is one big Hadley cell from the equator to each pole.
items.random_wrong: The three cells are tropical, temperate and arctic, named after climate zones.
items.why: Students often learn 'Hadley' as the only cell. There are three in each hemisphere, and their boundaries (at 30 and 60 degrees) explain global pressure belts, deserts and the polar front.
The answer examiners want is: Open systems. “Closed systems” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximate Holderness cliff retreat rate is:
items.why: It's 1.8 m/yr. “0.18 m/yr” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Annual sediment delivery from UK rivers in million tonnes:
items.why: The real figure is 10. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
